era_context: The Iron Age II Levant, approximately two years after Qarqar. Shalmaneser III's reign continues. The Levantine coalition remains a significant regional force. In Israel, Ahab's reign is nearing its end (traditional date of death: 852 BCE). Egypt under the 22nd Dynasty remains in decline. The Greek world is in the Geometric period. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is in its final decades before the traditional collapse date of 771 BCE.

events.title: Death of Ahab of Israel (traditional date)
events.description: According to biblical tradition, Ahab died in 852 BCE, ending a reign of approximately 20–22 years. The Hebrew Bible describes his death in battle against the Arameans at Ramoth-Gilead, though the historical accuracy of this account is debated. His death marked a succession crisis in Israel and the beginning of the reign of his son Ahaziah. The timing of Ahab's death coincides with a period of continued Assyrian pressure on the Levant, which would intensify under his successors.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus based on regnal chronology

events.certainty_note: The date of Ahab's death is derived from biblical tradition and scholarly reconstruction of Israelite chronology. No contemporary Assyrian or other external source documents his death in this specific year. Some scholars place it 1–2 years earlier or later. The biblical narrative is a later compilation (post-exilic), and the account of his death in battle may be legendary or symbolic.

events.title: Succession of Ahaziah in Israel
events.description: Following Ahab's death, his son Ahaziah became king of Israel. Ahaziah's reign was brief (traditionally dated 852–851 BCE), and he is portrayed in the Hebrew Bible as continuing his father's religious policies and facing challenges from both internal prophetic opposition (the prophet Elijah) and external military threats. His reign marks a period of transition in Israel and a shift in the balance of power in the Levant as Assyrian pressure continued to mount.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus based on regnal chronology

events.certainty_note: Ahaziah's reign is attested in biblical tradition but not in contemporary external sources. The dating is derived from biblical chronology, which is debated among scholars. Some scholars place his reign 851–850 BCE or adjust the dates by several years.

events.title: Shalmaneser III's Continued Campaigns (ongoing)
events.description: Shalmaneser III continued his military campaigns during this period, consolidating Assyrian control and maintaining pressure on the Levantine states. While no specific campaign is documented for 852 BCE in surviving sources, the pattern of Assyrian expansion during Shalmaneser III's reign suggests ongoing military activity. The death of Ahab and succession of Ahaziah in Israel may have been perceived as a moment of weakness by the Assyrians, though no immediate conquest followed.

sources.name: Historiographic inference from the pattern of Shalmaneser III's reign

events.certainty_note: No specific campaign is documented for 852 BCE, but the broader pattern of Shalmaneser III's military activity during his reign (858–824 BCE) suggests ongoing campaigns. The year is assigned by convention based on regnal chronology.

disconfirming_evidence: The biblical account of Ahab's death in battle at Ramoth-Gilead is not corroborated by external sources and may be legendary or symbolic. Some scholars argue that the biblical chronology for Ahab and his successors is off by several years, placing his death in 850 BCE or later. The specific dating of Ahaziah's reign is also disputed, with some scholars placing it 851–850 BCE or adjusting the dates based on different chronological systems.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 852 BCE is dominated by biblical tradition, which is a later compilation and reflects the perspective of the Judean scribal tradition. No contemporary Assyrian sources document the death of Ahab or the succession of Ahaziah. Archaeological evidence from Iron Age II sites provides material context but does not directly date these political events. The record is heavily skewed toward the Levant and Mesopotamia; events in other regions are either undocumented or known only through much later sources. The reliability of the biblical chronology for this period is a matter of ongoing scholarly debate.

graph_edges.note: Ahab's reign ended with his death in 852 BCE
Ahab's death led to the succession of Ahaziah
Ahaziah's brief reign was followed by the succession of Jehoram
